interrogator_dossier:
# Leggett & Platt (LEG) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Transcripts reviewed: Q4-2024 (2025-02-14), Q1-2025 (2025-04-29), Q2-2025 (2025-08-01), Q3-2025 (2025-10-28), Q4-2025 (2026-02-12). Financials per screener scan (FY2025) + FMP TTM as of 2026-03-31. **Critical out-of-transcript event: on 2026-04-13, after the last call in this set, Somnigroup International (NYSE: SGI) agreed to acquire LEG in an all-stock merger at a fixed 0.1455 exchange ratio — see §6/§8.***

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these five quarters is remarkably consistent and, crucially, **defensive throughout** — a self-help turnaround inside a multi-year residential demand depression, not a growth story.

- **Q4-2024** opened the arc with the "year of significant change" framing: a restructuring plan (launched early 2024) that "exceeded our expectation" of $10–15M EBIT benefit (delivered $22M), with the headline strategic teaser being *"the exploration of a sale of our aerospace business."* The forward posture was already pure damage-control: *"we expect our demand to remain under pressure as existing home sales remain near multi-decade lows."*
- **Q1-2025** sharpened two threads: (1) the aerospace sale moved from "exploration" to *"we recently signed an agreement to sell our aerospace business and expect to receive after-tax cash proceeds of approximately $240 million"*, and (2) tariffs entered as the dominant macro variable, framed as *"in aggregate, likely a net positive for us"* (steel 232 helps innersprings/rod-and-wire; adjustable bed is the exposed loser).
- **Q2-2025** held the line — restructuring "now largely complete," aerospace "on track to close this year," debt paydown elevated to a top-three priority. Tariff optimism on finished-mattress import enforcement intensified (*"greater than 50% [of imports] have come from Indonesia… transshipment… 40% penalty"*).
- **Q3-2025** was the pivot to **balance-sheet victory**: aerospace closed Aug-29, **all commercial paper retired**, net-debt/EBITDA cut to 2.6x, debt down $296M in the quarter / $367M YTD. The narrative shifted from "executing restructuring" to "we have now de-levered and are positioned to accelerate when residential turns."
- **Q4-2025 (Feb-2026)** is the capstone: restructuring "substantially complete," $60–70M annualized EBIT benefit "at lower cost than originally expected," and a forward pitch of *"return capital to shareholders"* and being *"well positioned to capitalize on the incremental volume when it materializes."* The 2026 guide is the first flat-ish guide in years (Bedding down LSD, Specialized flat-to-up ex-Aero).

**Drivers introduced:** trade rod & wire / metal-margin expansion (the genuine 2025 bright spot), semi-finished bedding content (Quantum/ComfortCore/EcoBase), Textiles geo-components, Work Furniture share gains, Vietnam home-furniture greenfield.
**Drivers quietly dropped:** aerospace (sold), and any pretense of top-line growth — every call walks revenue *down*.

The through-line: management executed a credible cost-and-balance-sheet rescue but never once produced organic growth. **The story was always "survive the depression and de-lever," and it succeeded at exactly that — which is precisely what made LEG an attractive *acquisition target* rather than a standalone re-rater.** That this set of transcripts ends in Feb-2026 with zero hint of a sale, yet a definitive all-stock merger was signed eight weeks later (Apr-13-2026), tells you the strategic-review optionality management kept emphasizing ("we will continue to look at every business to see if we're the rightful owner") ultimately resolved at the *whole-company* level.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | The number | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Significant progress strengthening the balance sheet / deleveraging" | Q3/Q4-25 | Debt cut $367M YTD to $1.5B; CP retired; net-debt/EBITDA 2.6x. But live BS (2026-03-31) still shows **net debt $1.14B** (total debt $1.65B − cash $0.51B) | **TRUE and the single most credible claim.** Real, auditable deleveraging — the spine of the bull case. |
| "Restructuring delivered greater EBIT benefit at lower cost than expected" ($60–70M annualized) | Q3/Q4-25 | Adjusted EBIT *still fell* $4M YoY to $263M in 2025; the benefit offset volume loss rather than growing earnings | **PARTLY TRUE but oversold.** The savings are real; they merely kept adjusted EBIT roughly flat against −7% revenue. Cost-out is treading water, not creating value. |
| "Tariffs are, in aggregate, a net positive for us" | Q1–Q3-25 | Metal margin expansion is visible (cited repeatedly as the offset); but TTM FCF collapsed to **$207M** and latest-Q EPS −36% YoY | **UNPROVEN / NET WASH at best.** The metal-margin tailwind is real but small; demand destruction and adjustable-bed disadvantage are the larger forces. The "net positive" claim is more hope than P&L evidence. |
| "Improved cash flow generation" | Q4-25 | 2025 operating cash flow $338M (+$33M); but **TTM FCF only $207M** and the +25.4% YoY FCF "growth" is off a depressed base | **MIXED.** OCF improved on working-capital harvest ($83M benefit in 2025, explicitly *not* repeatable — Burns: "not anticipating any working capital benefit in 2026"). FCF quality is weak. |
| "Positioned to accelerate when residential turns" | every call | Revenue: 5.15B (’22) → 4.73B → 4.38B → **4.06B (’25)**; 3-yr CAGR −7.6%; FY25 Q1-26 actual sales $918M (−10%) | **UNFALSIFIABLE / NO EVIDENCE YET.** Four straight years of decline. The "turn" has never arrived in the data; it is a permanent forward promise. |
| "Return capital to shareholders" | Q4-25 | Dividend was slashed to **$0.05/quarter** (the screener flags a "dramatic dividend cut"); $0.05 declared for Q1 and Q2 2026 | **TECHNICALLY TRUE, materially gutted.** This is a token $0.20 annualized dividend on a stock that was a Dividend King — the "return capital" line is rhetorical cover for a destroyed payout. |
| EPS recovery | Q4-25 | GAAP FY25 EPS $1.69 (vs −$3.73 in ’24) but **adjusted EPS $1.05, flat YoY**; TTM diluted EPS $1.61, Q1-26 adjusted EPS $0.15 (−38% vs cons) | **THE GAAP HEADLINE FLATTERS.** The $1.69 is a swing off a goodwill-impairment base; underlying adjusted earnings power is ~$1.05 and *falling* into 2026. |

**Net:** the financials corroborate the *balance-sheet* and *cost-discipline* claims and refute the *earnings-power* and *return-to-growth* claims. The narrative is **poorer than the de-leveraging story but richer than the operating trajectory** — i.e., management won the war it could control (debt) and is losing the one it can't (residential demand).

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: STABLE-to-mildly-rising on execution confidence; persistently DEFENSIVE on demand.** This is a management team that grew more confident about *what it controls* while never claiming visibility on the top line.

- **Q4-2024 (defensive):** *"another year of demand pressure"*; *"our biggest concern… relative poor health of the consumer… we have a concern that it could get worse."* Glassman openly names consumer confidence as the swing factor he can't influence.
- **Q1-2025 (cautiously constructive on self-help):** *"pleased to report better-than-anticipated first quarter earnings… a testament to the excellent execution of our restructuring plan."* First sign of earnings confidence.
- **Q2-2025 (rising on balance sheet):** *"I am pleased we grew our earnings versus last year and continue to strengthen our balance sheet."* SG&A leverage called out for the first time in years.
- **Q3-2025 (peak self-help confidence):** *"another quarter of solid results… these efforts have culminated in improved cash flow, which allowed us to significantly strengthen our balance sheet."* This is the most confident call in the set.
- **Q4-2025 (steady, forward-leaning but hedged):** *"we delivered on those priorities… these improvements are sustainable."* Yet immediately hedges on demand: *"residential markets… remain in a multiyear depression with demand well below average cycle levels"* and flags weather drag on Q1-26.

The language axis is unmistakably **offensive on cost/efficiency, defensive on growth** — exactly the profile of a late-stage self-help story that has wrung out the controllable upside and is now waiting on a macro it cannot summon. Guidance got *more* specific over time (segment-level 2026 guide), which is a credibility positive.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-HIGH on the controllables, LOW on demand forecasting.**

- **Restructuring guidance was serially beaten:** initial $10–15M EBIT benefit → delivered $22M (Q4-24); sales attrition guided $40M → came in $15M then ~$60M but with *better* EBIT; real-estate proceeds guided $0–10M → $20M realized, then raised to $70–80M plan-total. They consistently under-promised and over-delivered on the self-help plan. **This is genuine sandbagging in the company's favor — a credibility green flag.**
- **Aerospace proceeds:** guided ~$240M after-tax (Q1-25) → closed at ~$250M after-tax (Aug-29). **Delivered as promised.**
- **Demand guidance was repeatedly walked down within the year:** Bedding 2025 guided "down mid-single digits," then production landed "down high single digits"; the mattress "inflection" was promised every quarter and never materialized. The screener's **beat rate is only 4/7 quarters (57%)** and Q1-2026 adjusted EPS missed consensus by **38%** ($0.15 vs ~$0.24) — confirming demand forecasting is unreliable.

Reconciling: the 57% beat rate + the post-transcript Q1-26 miss caps overall reliability at **Medium**. Management is trustworthy on cost execution and asset sales (things they control), and unreliable on end-market timing (things they don't).

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"When does residential turn?"** — asked in some form on every call (Maklari, Keith, Morgan). Management never answers; the standard deflection is *"we cannot predict the timing of demand recovery, [but] we are well positioned."* Four years running. This is the central unanswered question.
- **The "certain customer" headwind** (Mattress Firm private-label insourcing + an adjustable-bed merchandising change) — Hagale was pushed repeatedly (Q2, Q3-25) on *when it laps*; the answer kept sliding: *"it will really take fully through next year before we'll fully get through that part of it"* (Q3-25). A serially-extended drag.
- **FF&T margin collapse in Q4-25** — Maklari pressed hard on the *"meaningful drop-off in their margin"*; Smith's answer was a laundry list (flooring pricing, currency, Vietnam greenfield start-up, demand) — diffuse, not crisp. A soft spot.
- **Mattress import enforcement / tariffs** — analysts (Keith, Hughes) probed whether tariff "level playing field" optimism would convert to volume; by Q3-25 management admitted *"we have not yet seen a noticeable improvement in our innerspring demand."* The tariff thesis was talked up for three quarters and had not shown up in the numbers.
- **Notably absent:** no analyst on any of these calls asked about a *whole-company sale*. The strategic review was always framed (and questioned) at the *business-line* level. The Somnigroup deal blindsided the standalone narrative.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🟢 **Real, audited deleveraging** — CP retired, net-debt/EBITDA 2.6x, $367M debt cut in 2025 (Q3/Q4-25). The most credible thread in the dossier.
🟢 **Serial under-promise/over-deliver on restructuring** — $60–70M EBIT benefit at lower cost than guided; aerospace proceeds delivered ~$250M as promised (§4).
🟢 **Genuine bright spots:** trade rod & wire metal-margin expansion, semi-finished bedding content gains ("Quantum share… semi-finished business grew more than 20%," Q3-25), Textiles geo-components, Work Furniture share gains.
🟢 **Specific, segment-level 2026 guide** — increasing transparency, not less.

🔴 **Four consecutive years of revenue decline** (5.15B→4.06B) with **3-yr CAGR −7.6%**; Q1-2026 sales −10%. No organic growth anywhere in the record.
🔴 **Dividend gutted to $0.05/qtr** — a former Dividend King reduced to a token payout; "return capital to shareholders" is rhetorical.
🔴 **Adjusted EPS flat-to-down** ($1.05 FY25, Q1-26 adj $0.15 −38% vs cons) — cost-out is only offsetting volume loss, not building earnings power.
🔴 **TTM FCF only $207M** with a non-repeatable working-capital benefit flagged as gone for 2026.
🔴 **"Net positive tariffs" claim unproven** in the P&L after three quarters of promotion; adjustable-bed business is structurally disadvantaged and being consolidated to Mexico.
🔴 **ROE −3.6%, ROIC 8.0%** — sub-cost-of-capital returns; this is a low-quality, cyclically-impaired industrial.
🔴 **Zero open-market insider buying** (per live correction) despite a stock at ~5x P/FCF — management put no personal capital behind the "well positioned" rhetoric.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **IR churn is conspicuous:** the IR host changed *four times* across five calls — Cassie Branscum (Q4-24, Q1-25) → Steve West (Q2, Q3-25) → Ryan Kleiboeker (Q4-25). Branscum was *promoted* to VP of FP&A (a "critical role in the formation and execution of our financial strategy," Q2-25). Heavy finance-org reshuffling is consistent with a company preparing for a balance-sheet/strategic endgame.
- **Vocabulary shift from "restructuring" → "optimization" → "return capital":** the lexicon moved from active surgery (2024) to maintenance + capital-return framing (Q4-25), the classic arc of a self-help story reaching the end of its runway — at which point the value-creating move becomes a *transaction*, not more operations.
- **"We will continue to look at every business to see if we're the rightful owner"** (Glassman, Q1-25) — repeated as an open-ended portfolio-review mandate. Management telegraphed willingness to sell *anything*; the market read it as piecemeal divestiture, but it logically extended to selling the *whole*.
- **Somnigroup is a customer.** The merger rationale (per the Apr-2026 announcement) is *vertical integration* — Somnigroup (Tempur Sealy + Mattress Firm + Dreams) buying its key component supplier. LEG management's repeated emphasis on "semi-finished content gains with leading OEMs" was, in hindsight, deepening exactly the customer relationship that produced the buyer. The strategic logic was visible in the transcripts; the transaction was not.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING — and the pivot has already resolved into a fixed-terms all-stock takeout.**

Through the transcript window, LEG was a **STABLE-to-strengthening self-help/de-leveraging story** with a deteriorating top line — a textbook "cheap, impaired, but balance-sheet-fixed deep-value" name (5.3x P/FCF, DCF MoS +59%). Management did the controllable work well: retired CP, cut net debt to 2.6x EBITDA, beat restructuring targets, sold aerospace for ~$250M as promised. What they could *not* do is grow — four straight years of decline, sub-cost-of-capital returns, a gutted dividend, and zero insider conviction.

**The single most important thing the director must weigh:** *the standalone deep-value thesis is now moot — LEG is a definitive merger-arb situation, not an intrinsic-value re-rater.* On **2026-04-13** Somnigroup agreed to acquire LEG in an **all-stock deal at a fixed 0.1455 SGI-per-LEG exchange ratio** (~14% premium to the Apr-10 close, ~$2.5B total, ~9% pro-forma LEG ownership, expected close by year-end 2026, unanimously approved by both boards, $64M LEG break fee / $80M SGI regulatory fee). This means:

interrogator_dossier:
# Leggett & Platt (LEG) — Interrogator Forensic Dossier

*Transcripts reviewed: Q4-2024 (2025-02-14), Q1-2025 (2025-04-29), Q2-2025 (2025-08-01), Q3-2025 (2025-10-28), Q4-2025 (2026-02-12). Financials per screener scan (FY2025) + FMP TTM as of 2026-03-31. **Critical out-of-transcript event: on 2026-04-13, after the last call in this set, Somnigroup International (NYSE: SGI) agreed to acquire LEG in an all-stock merger at a fixed 0.1455 exchange ratio — see §6/§8.***

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these five quarters is remarkably consistent and, crucially, **defensive throughout** — a self-help turnaround inside a multi-year residential demand depression, not a growth story.

- **Q4-2024** opened the arc with the "year of significant change" framing: a restructuring plan (launched early 2024) that "exceeded our expectation" of $10–15M EBIT benefit (delivered $22M), with the headline strategic teaser being *"the exploration of a sale of our aerospace business."* The forward posture was already pure damage-control: *"we expect our demand to remain under pressure as existing home sales remain near multi-decade lows."*
- **Q1-2025** sharpened two threads: (1) the aerospace sale moved from "exploration" to *"we recently signed an agreement to sell our aerospace business and expect to receive after-tax cash proceeds of approximately $240 million"*, and (2) tariffs entered as the dominant macro variable, framed as *"in aggregate, likely a net positive for us"* (steel 232 helps innersprings/rod-and-wire; adjustable bed is the exposed loser).
- **Q2-2025** held the line — restructuring "now largely complete," aerospace "on track to close this year," debt paydown elevated to a top-three priority. Tariff optimism on finished-mattress import enforcement intensified (*"greater than 50% [of imports] have come from Indonesia… transshipment… 40% penalty"*).
- **Q3-2025** was the pivot to **balance-sheet victory**: aerospace closed Aug-29, **all commercial paper retired**, net-debt/EBITDA cut to 2.6x, debt down $296M in the quarter / $367M YTD. The narrative shifted from "executing restructuring" to "we have now de-levered and are positioned to accelerate when residential turns."
- **Q4-2025 (Feb-2026)** is the capstone: restructuring "substantially complete," $60–70M annualized EBIT benefit "at lower cost than originally expected," and a forward pitch of *"return capital to shareholders"* and being *"well positioned to capitalize on the incremental volume when it materializes."* The 2026 guide is the first flat-ish guide in years (Bedding down LSD, Specialized flat-to-up ex-Aero).

**Drivers introduced:** trade rod & wire / metal-margin expansion (the genuine 2025 bright spot), semi-finished bedding content (Quantum/ComfortCore/EcoBase), Textiles geo-components, Work Furniture share gains, Vietnam home-furniture greenfield.
**Drivers quietly dropped:** aerospace (sold), and any pretense of top-line growth — every call walks revenue *down*.

The through-line: management executed a credible cost-and-balance-sheet rescue but never once produced organic growth. **The story was always "survive the depression and de-lever," and it succeeded at exactly that — which is precisely what made LEG an attractive *acquisition target* rather than a standalone re-rater.** That this set of transcripts ends in Feb-2026 with zero hint of a sale, yet a definitive all-stock merger was signed eight weeks later (Apr-13-2026), tells you the strategic-review optionality management kept emphasizing ("we will continue to look at every business to see if we're the rightful owner") ultimately resolved at the *whole-company* level.

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | The number | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Significant progress strengthening the balance sheet / deleveraging" | Q3/Q4-25 | Debt cut $367M YTD to $1.5B; CP retired; net-debt/EBITDA 2.6x. But live BS (2026-03-31) still shows **net debt $1.14B** (total debt $1.65B − cash $0.51B) | **TRUE and the single most credible claim.** Real, auditable deleveraging — the spine of the bull case. |
| "Restructuring delivered greater EBIT benefit at lower cost than expected" ($60–70M annualized) | Q3/Q4-25 | Adjusted EBIT *still fell* $4M YoY to $263M in 2025; the benefit offset volume loss rather than growing earnings | **PARTLY TRUE but oversold.** The savings are real; they merely kept adjusted EBIT roughly flat against −7% revenue. Cost-out is treading water, not creating value. |
| "Tariffs are, in aggregate, a net positive for us" | Q1–Q3-25 | Metal margin expansion is visible (cited repeatedly as the offset); but TTM FCF collapsed to **$207M** and latest-Q EPS −36% YoY | **UNPROVEN / NET WASH at best.** The metal-margin tailwind is real but small; demand destruction and adjustable-bed disadvantage are the larger forces. The "net positive" claim is more hope than P&L evidence. |
| "Improved cash flow generation" | Q4-25 | 2025 operating cash flow $338M (+$33M); but **TTM FCF only $207M** and the +25.4% YoY FCF "growth" is off a depressed base | **MIXED.** OCF improved on working-capital harvest ($83M benefit in 2025, explicitly *not* repeatable — Burns: "not anticipating any working capital benefit in 2026"). FCF quality is weak. |
| "Positioned to accelerate when residential turns" | every call | Revenue: 5.15B (’22) → 4.73B → 4.38B → **4.06B (’25)**; 3-yr CAGR −7.6%; FY25 Q1-26 actual sales $918M (−10%) | **UNFALSIFIABLE / NO EVIDENCE YET.** Four straight years of decline. The "turn" has never arrived in the data; it is a permanent forward promise. |
| "Return capital to shareholders" | Q4-25 | Dividend was slashed to **$0.05/quarter** (the screener flags a "dramatic dividend cut"); $0.05 declared for Q1 and Q2 2026 | **TECHNICALLY TRUE, materially gutted.** This is a token $0.20 annualized dividend on a stock that was a Dividend King — the "return capital" line is rhetorical cover for a destroyed payout. |
| EPS recovery | Q4-25 | GAAP FY25 EPS $1.69 (vs −$3.73 in ’24) but **adjusted EPS $1.05, flat YoY**; TTM diluted EPS $1.61, Q1-26 adjusted EPS $0.15 (−38% vs cons) | **THE GAAP HEADLINE FLATTERS.** The $1.69 is a swing off a goodwill-impairment base; underlying adjusted earnings power is ~$1.05 and *falling* into 2026. |

**Net:** the financials corroborate the *balance-sheet* and *cost-discipline* claims and refute the *earnings-power* and *return-to-growth* claims. The narrative is **poorer than the de-leveraging story but richer than the operating trajectory** — i.e., management won the war it could control (debt) and is losing the one it can't (residential demand).

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: STABLE-to-mildly-rising on execution confidence; persistently DEFENSIVE on demand.** This is a management team that grew more confident about *what it controls* while never claiming visibility on the top line.

- **Q4-2024 (defensive):** *"another year of demand pressure"*; *"our biggest concern… relative poor health of the consumer… we have a concern that it could get worse."* Glassman openly names consumer confidence as the swing factor he can't influence.
- **Q1-2025 (cautiously constructive on self-help):** *"pleased to report better-than-anticipated first quarter earnings… a testament to the excellent execution of our restructuring plan."* First sign of earnings confidence.
- **Q2-2025 (rising on balance sheet):** *"I am pleased we grew our earnings versus last year and continue to strengthen our balance sheet."* SG&A leverage called out for the first time in years.
- **Q3-2025 (peak self-help confidence):** *"another quarter of solid results… these efforts have culminated in improved cash flow, which allowed us to significantly strengthen our balance sheet."* This is the most confident call in the set.
- **Q4-2025 (steady, forward-leaning but hedged):** *"we delivered on those priorities… these improvements are sustainable."* Yet immediately hedges on demand: *"residential markets… remain in a multiyear depression with demand well below average cycle levels"* and flags weather drag on Q1-26.

The language axis is unmistakably **offensive on cost/efficiency, defensive on growth** — exactly the profile of a late-stage self-help story that has wrung out the controllable upside and is now waiting on a macro it cannot summon. Guidance got *more* specific over time (segment-level 2026 guide), which is a credibility positive.

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-HIGH on the controllables, LOW on demand forecasting.**

- **Restructuring guidance was serially beaten:** initial $10–15M EBIT benefit → delivered $22M (Q4-24); sales attrition guided $40M → came in $15M then ~$60M but with *better* EBIT; real-estate proceeds guided $0–10M → $20M realized, then raised to $70–80M plan-total. They consistently under-promised and over-delivered on the self-help plan. **This is genuine sandbagging in the company's favor — a credibility green flag.**
- **Aerospace proceeds:** guided ~$240M after-tax (Q1-25) → closed at ~$250M after-tax (Aug-29). **Delivered as promised.**
- **Demand guidance was repeatedly walked down within the year:** Bedding 2025 guided "down mid-single digits," then production landed "down high single digits"; the mattress "inflection" was promised every quarter and never materialized. The screener's **beat rate is only 4/7 quarters (57%)** and Q1-2026 adjusted EPS missed consensus by **38%** ($0.15 vs ~$0.24) — confirming demand forecasting is unreliable.

Reconciling: the 57% beat rate + the post-transcript Q1-26 miss caps overall reliability at **Medium**. Management is trustworthy on cost execution and asset sales (things they control), and unreliable on end-market timing (things they don't).

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **"When does residential turn?"** — asked in some form on every call (Maklari, Keith, Morgan). Management never answers; the standard deflection is *"we cannot predict the timing of demand recovery, [but] we are well positioned."* Four years running. This is the central unanswered question.
- **The "certain customer" headwind** (Mattress Firm private-label insourcing + an adjustable-bed merchandising change) — Hagale was pushed repeatedly (Q2, Q3-25) on *when it laps*; the answer kept sliding: *"it will really take fully through next year before we'll fully get through that part of it"* (Q3-25). A serially-extended drag.
- **FF&T margin collapse in Q4-25** — Maklari pressed hard on the *"meaningful drop-off in their margin"*; Smith's answer was a laundry list (flooring pricing, currency, Vietnam greenfield start-up, demand) — diffuse, not crisp. A soft spot.
- **Mattress import enforcement / tariffs** — analysts (Keith, Hughes) probed whether tariff "level playing field" optimism would convert to volume; by Q3-25 management admitted *"we have not yet seen a noticeable improvement in our innerspring demand."* The tariff thesis was talked up for three quarters and had not shown up in the numbers.
- **Notably absent:** no analyst on any of these calls asked about a *whole-company sale*. The strategic review was always framed (and questioned) at the *business-line* level. The Somnigroup deal blindsided the standalone narrative.

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🟢 **Real, audited deleveraging** — CP retired, net-debt/EBITDA 2.6x, $367M debt cut in 2025 (Q3/Q4-25). The most credible thread in the dossier.
🟢 **Serial under-promise/over-deliver on restructuring** — $60–70M EBIT benefit at lower cost than guided; aerospace proceeds delivered ~$250M as promised (§4).
🟢 **Genuine bright spots:** trade rod & wire metal-margin expansion, semi-finished bedding content gains ("Quantum share… semi-finished business grew more than 20%," Q3-25), Textiles geo-components, Work Furniture share gains.
🟢 **Specific, segment-level 2026 guide** — increasing transparency, not less.

🔴 **Four consecutive years of revenue decline** (5.15B→4.06B) with **3-yr CAGR −7.6%**; Q1-2026 sales −10%. No organic growth anywhere in the record.
🔴 **Dividend gutted to $0.05/qtr** — a former Dividend King reduced to a token payout; "return capital to shareholders" is rhetorical.
🔴 **Adjusted EPS flat-to-down** ($1.05 FY25, Q1-26 adj $0.15 −38% vs cons) — cost-out is only offsetting volume loss, not building earnings power.
🔴 **TTM FCF only $207M** with a non-repeatable working-capital benefit flagged as gone for 2026.
🔴 **"Net positive tariffs" claim unproven** in the P&L after three quarters of promotion; adjustable-bed business is structurally disadvantaged and being consolidated to Mexico.
🔴 **ROE −3.6%, ROIC 8.0%** — sub-cost-of-capital returns; this is a low-quality, cyclically-impaired industrial.
🔴 **Zero open-market insider buying** (per live correction) despite a stock at ~5x P/FCF — management put no personal capital behind the "well positioned" rhetoric.

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **IR churn is conspicuous:** the IR host changed *four times* across five calls — Cassie Branscum (Q4-24, Q1-25) → Steve West (Q2, Q3-25) → Ryan Kleiboeker (Q4-25). Branscum was *promoted* to VP of FP&A (a "critical role in the formation and execution of our financial strategy," Q2-25). Heavy finance-org reshuffling is consistent with a company preparing for a balance-sheet/strategic endgame.
- **Vocabulary shift from "restructuring" → "optimization" → "return capital":** the lexicon moved from active surgery (2024) to maintenance + capital-return framing (Q4-25), the classic arc of a self-help story reaching the end of its runway — at which point the value-creating move becomes a *transaction*, not more operations.
- **"We will continue to look at every business to see if we're the rightful owner"** (Glassman, Q1-25) — repeated as an open-ended portfolio-review mandate. Management telegraphed willingness to sell *anything*; the market read it as piecemeal divestiture, but it logically extended to selling the *whole*.
- **Somnigroup is a customer.** The merger rationale (per the Apr-2026 announcement) is *vertical integration* — Somnigroup (Tempur Sealy + Mattress Firm + Dreams) buying its key component supplier. LEG management's repeated emphasis on "semi-finished content gains with leading OEMs" was, in hindsight, deepening exactly the customer relationship that produced the buyer. The strategic logic was visible in the transcripts; the transaction was not.

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: PIVOTING — and the pivot has already resolved into a fixed-terms all-stock takeout.**

Through the transcript window, LEG was a **STABLE-to-strengthening self-help/de-leveraging story** with a deteriorating top line — a textbook "cheap, impaired, but balance-sheet-fixed deep-value" name (5.3x P/FCF, DCF MoS +59%). Management did the controllable work well: retired CP, cut net debt to 2.6x EBITDA, beat restructuring targets, sold aerospace for ~$250M as promised. What they could *not* do is grow — four straight years of decline, sub-cost-of-capital returns, a gutted dividend, and zero insider conviction.

interrogator_dossier:
# Leggett & Platt (LEG) — Forensic Dossier
*Interrogator | 5 consecutive quarterly calls (Q4-24 reported 2025-02-14 → Q4-25 reported 2026-02-12) cross-referenced against the screener trajectory*

---

## 1. NARRATIVE ARC

The story across these five calls is a single, coherent arc: **"from defense to durability"** — a self-help restructuring story bolted on top of a multi-year residential demand depression that management cannot fix and does not pretend to.

- **Q4-24 (2025-02-14):** The frame is *survival and restructuring*. Karl Glassman opens on "a year of significant change," $22M of restructuring EBIT benefit "exceeding our expectation," 14 bedding locations closed, US innerspring consolidation "with zero customer interruptions." Demand "expected to remain under pressure as existing home sales remain near multi-decade lows." First mention of the strategic lever that becomes the year's spine: *"the exploration of a sale of our aerospace business."* The narrative is almost entirely cost-out + portfolio-pruning; growth is deferred to "a multiyear recovery… eventually."
- **Q1-25 (2025-04-29):** Two new pillars appear. (1) **Aerospace is now signed** — "we recently signed an agreement to sell our aerospace business and expect to receive after-tax cash proceeds of approximately $240 million." (2) **Tariffs reframed as a net positive** — "in aggregate, are likely a net positive for us," with steel 232 tariffs "leading to expanded metal margins and higher demand for our steel rod and drawn wire operations." Tone shifts from pure defense to "better-than-anticipated first quarter earnings."
- **Q2-25 (2025-08-01):** Consolidation. Restructuring "now largely complete," aerospace "on track to close this year." Tariff optimism persists ("net positive… given what we know today") but with hedges — "enforcement remains a key unknown." A new structural negative crystallizes: the *Mattress Firm specialty-foam private-label loss + adjustable-bed merchandising change* — "we expect these merchandising changes will remain as headwinds through the remainder of the year." SG&A leverage appears "for the first time in several years."
- **Q3-25 (2025-10-28):** The deleveraging payoff. Aerospace **closed Aug 29**; proceeds + cash "paid down all of our remaining commercial paper," net-debt/EBITDA cut to 2.6x (0.3x higher ex-aero on a pro forma basis), $296M debt reduction in the quarter alone. Bedding "improved 3% sequentially… second consecutive quarter of improved domestic production." First constructive enforcement data point: "US Customs… dismantled a Chinese mattress transshipment scheme estimated to have evaded more than $400 million in duties."
- **Q4-25 (2026-02-12):** Victory-lap-on-the-balance-sheet, caution-on-demand. "We delivered on those priorities… substantially completed the restructuring plan." Residential markets "remain in a multiyear depression with demand well below average cycle levels." 2026 guide: Bedding "down low single digits," Specialized "down 1% to 2%," with the recovery explicitly *un-dated*: "we cannot predict the timing of demand recovery." The forward story is now almost entirely operating leverage on an eventual volume return — "well positioned to capitalize on the incremental volume when it materializes."

**Quietly dropped / never re-litigated:** the *dividend*. It is conspicuously absent from every transcript. The 52-year aristocrat streak was already cut in 2024 (pre-window); management never volunteers a path back, and only lists "dividends" last in a generic capital-allocation priority list (Q4-25). **Quietly added:** Vietnam home-furniture greenfield, medical nonwovens in Textiles, GEO components share gains at home-improvement retailers — small, real, but not needle-movers against a $4B base.

---

## 2. CLAIMS vs FINANCIALS (forensic)

| Mgmt claim | Quarter | What the financials say | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| "Restructuring delivered greater EBIT benefit at lower cost than expected" ($60–70M annualized) | Q3/Q4-25 | Adjusted EBIT *fell* $4M YoY to $263M in 2025; adjusted EPS flat at $1.05. The benefit is real but **fully consumed by volume decline** — it is offsetting erosion, not adding. | **TRUE but net-zero.** Self-help is treading water against the demand sink. |
| "Strengthen the balance sheet… meaningfully closer to 2x leverage" | every Q | Net debt cut from 3.8x→2.4x EBITDA (Q4-25); debt down $376M FY; CP retired to zero. Screener confirms net debt ~1.14B (down from prior). | **TRUE and the strongest claim in the book.** Deleveraging is genuine and on-plan. |
| "Tariffs are a net positive for us" | Q1–Q3-25 | Metal-margin expansion is visible (offsets residential weakness); but revenue still fell -7% FY and the adjustable-bed business was *disadvantaged* enough that they **closed the Kentucky plant** and moved it to Mexico. | **MIXED.** Net-positive on metal margin, net-negative on adjustable bed + consumer demand. Self-aware hedging ("concerned tariffs drive inflation, hurt consumer confidence"). |
| "Multiyear recovery… well positioned when volume materializes" | every Q | Revenue trajectory: 5.07B→5.15B→4.73B→4.38B→4.06B. EPS: 2.94→2.27→(1.00)→(3.73)→1.69. **5-yr EPS CAGR -12.9%, BVPS CAGR -11.7%.** No inflection in the actuals. | **UNPROVEN / deferred indefinitely.** The recovery is a permanent "next year" — 4 consecutive years of decline, no date offered. |
| Consensus fwd EPS growth +15.7% | screener | TTM diluted EPS $1.61, latest-Q EPS YoY **-36%**; 2026 adjusted-EPS guide $1.00–1.20 = **flat-to-down vs 2025's $1.05 adjusted**. | **STREET IS OFFSIDE HIGH.** The +15.7% fwd growth is not supported by management's own flat 2026 guide. |
| ROE / returns | screener | ROE -3.6% (dragged by 2024 $676M goodwill impairment); ROIC 8.0%. Gross margin 18.1% "stable." | **WEAK-BUT-STABILIZING.** ROIC ~8% ≈ WACC; this is a no-economic-profit business, not a compounder. |

**Where the story is richer than the numbers:** the "positioning for profitable long-term growth" framing implies an inflection the trajectory has never shown. **Where it is poorer:** management *under*-sells the one genuinely good thing — the balance-sheet repair and ~5.4x P/FCF / 6.9% FCF yield are barely trumpeted, because trumpeting them invites the question "then why is the dividend still cut?"

---

## 3. TONE & CONFIDENCE TRAJECTORY

**Trajectory: STABLE (low-amplitude, defensively confident).** Management is neither deteriorating nor inflecting — they are steady-state competent operators in a bad market.

- Q4-24: defensive, grinding — *"a daunting macro environment"*, *"our asset base is so volume sensitive… we need volume."* (Glassman's candor about the consumer is a credibility green flag.)
- Q1-25: cautiously up — *"better-than-anticipated first quarter earnings."*
- Q2-25: steadiest — *"I am pleased we grew our earnings versus last year."* SG&A leverage "for the first time in several years."
- Q3-25: most confident on the balance sheet — *"another quarter of solid results… significantly strengthen our balance sheet"* — but explicitly *reaffirming the midpoint*, not raising.
- Q4-25: confident on execution, deliberately humble on demand — *"we cannot predict the timing of demand recovery"*, plus a fresh hedge: *"big weather events… certainly have had some impact on the business early in the [Q1-26] quarter."*

The language has shifted decisively from **offensive→defensive** over the multi-year arc (growth/innovation talk in 2021–22 has become efficiency/cost/footprint-optimization talk), but *within these five calls* the defensive posture is already fully set and stable. Guidance specificity is high and consistent.

---

## 4. GUIDANCE CREDIBILITY

**Reliability: MEDIUM-HIGH on what they control, LOW on demand timing.**

- Restructuring guidance was repeatedly **beaten**: 2024 EBIT benefit "$22M vs expectation of $10–15M"; sales attrition "$15M… well below our initial estimate of $40M"; real-estate proceeds "$20M… above initial $0–10M." This is a multi-quarter pattern of *under-promising and over-delivering on the self-help line* — a genuine sandbagging-on-controllables credibility builder.
- They reaffirmed the FY-2025 midpoint in Q3 and **hit** FY-2025 (adjusted EPS $1.05, in the guided range; sales $4.05B).
- The screener's "beat 4/7 qtrs (57%)" reflects the noise of demand misses dragging headline results — the *operational* guidance (cost, leverage, restructuring) is much more reliable than the 57% headline implies.
- **The persistent miss is demand:** every call forecasts stabilization/sequential improvement and every call the residential trough deepens or merely flattens. The 2026 guide ($3.8–4.0B, down 1–6%) is honest about this — no hockey stick.

Net: **management's word is good on the controllable line; treat their "recovery is coming" as directionally honest but un-bankable on timing.** Score: **MEDIUM-HIGH (3.5/5).**

---

## 5. ANALYST PRESSURE POINTS

- **Mattress import enforcement** — analysts (Keith Hughes, Peter Keith) returned to this every call. Management is *increasingly specific and increasingly hopeful* (transshipment crackdowns, de minimis, CPSC flammability, 75% of imports "do not meet US flammability laws"). This is the bull's load-bearing external catalyst — and it is **entirely outside management's control and serially deferred** ("enforcement remains a key unknown," Q2). Analysts keep probing because it never quite converts to volume.
- **Adjustable-bed / specialty-foam customer loss** — pushed hard in Q2/Q3. Management eventually named it (Mattress Firm private-label in-sourcing + a second large customer) and admitted it "will really take fully through next year before we'll fully get through that part of it" (Q3). A clean case of an analyst-flagged worry becoming confirmed bad news.
- **FF&T margin collapse in Q4-25** — Maklari pressed on "that meaningful drop-off in their margin." Sam Smith's answer was a laundry list (pricing adjustments in flooring/textiles, currency drag, Vietnam greenfield start-up costs, weak consumer) — partly transitory, partly "ongoing… until consumer demand turns around." A non-clean answer that signals the demand problem is bleeding into the previously-resilient segment.
- **The unasked question:** *no analyst on these calls pushed management on dividend reinstatement.* The sell-side has accepted the cut as permanent — itself a tell about how the shareholder base has already turned over.

---

## 6. RED FLAGS / GREEN FLAGS

🔴 **Revenue in secular/structural decline** — 5.15B→4.06B over four years, -7.4% latest, with management itself calling residential a "multiyear depression" and conceding domestic mattress production "down high single digits" in 2025. The 3-yr revenue CAGR is -7.6%.
🔴 **No dated demand catalyst, recovery explicitly un-forecastable** — "we cannot predict the timing of demand recovery" (Q4-25). The thesis depends on housing/consumer-confidence/affordability, none of which management influences.
🔴 **Latest-Q EPS -36% YoY; 2026 adjusted EPS guided flat-to-down** ($1.00–1.20 vs $1.05) — the self-help benefit has been fully harvested; there is no further cost lever to pull against continued volume erosion.
🔴 **FF&T (the resilient segment) margin broke in Q4-25** — demand weakness is spreading, not contained.
🔴 **Dividend cut never reversed; zero open-market insider buying** — neither management capital (insider buys) nor the board (dividend) is signaling conviction in a near-term turn.

🟢 **Balance-sheet repair is real and on-plan** — 3.8x→2.4x net-debt/EBITDA, CP to zero, $376M FY debt reduction, aerospace $250M after-tax proceeds banked. Altman-Z 3.1 = solvent.
🟢 **Restructuring over-delivered repeatedly** — $60–70M annualized EBIT benefit at lower cost than promised; metal-margin expansion from steel tariffs is a structural offset.
🟢 **Operating leverage is genuinely coiled** — "our asset base is so volume sensitive"; if/when residential turns, the incremental drop-through is high. This is the legitimate core of the bull case.
🟢 **Management candor** — Glassman's repeated honesty about consumer health and his refusal to forecast a recovery he can't see are credibility-positive, not evasive.

---

## 7. HIDDEN SIGNALS

- **IR-suite churn:** three different IR hosts across five calls (Cassie Branscum → Steve West → Ryan Kleiboeker), with Branscum *promoted* to VP of FP&A. Normal succession, but the volume of personnel motion (plus "new leadership in 2026" in Automotive) signals an org still being reshaped, not yet settled.
- **Terminology shift "growth → footprint optimization / cost reduction / portfolio simplification"** — the verbs are now almost entirely about *shrinking efficiently*. "Profitable long-term growth" is always paired with "when residential turns" — i.e. growth is outsourced to the macro.
- **Capital allocation re-prioritized away from the shareholder:** the de-facto priority is *debt paydown first* — "most excess cash flow in 2026 will be used to reduce net debt." Buybacks and dividend reinstatement are theoretical, not funded. The yield-investor base that owned this for 52 years has been structurally evicted.
- **"Net positive" tariff framing is load-bearing and fragile** — it rests on metal-margin expansion (real) + a hoped-for import-enforcement tailwind (un-converted). A change in steel-tariff policy or a consumer-demand crack from tariff-driven inflation flips it.
- **The strategic review is open-ended** — "it's ongoing… we will continue to look at every business" (Q1-25). Aerospace was the one clean divestiture; nothing else has been announced as for-sale, so the "more divestitures" optionality is real but undated and soft.

---

## 8. CAPITAL-ALLOCATION VERDICT (for the director)

**Classification: STABLE (operationally), tilting PIVOTING (portfolio).** This is a competently-run, structurally-challenged cyclical that has spent two years converting a balance-sheet emergency into a clean-ish balance sheet, while its core residential end-markets remain in a depression with no dated bottom. The self-help story is essentially *complete* — the easy cost-out is harvested, adjusted EBIT is flat, and from here the equity is a **levered bet on a US housing/mattress volume recovery that management explicitly refuses to time.**

**The single most important thing the director must weigh before sizing:** *The only hard, dated corporate catalyst in the file — the aerospace sale — has already FIRED (closed Aug-29-2025, proceeds spent on debt). What remains is (a) deleveraging that is two-thirds done and (b) a macro volume recovery with no date and no management control.* There is no forcing function on the horizon; this is a "cheap, solvent, secularly-soft cyclical waiting for a cycle," not a special-situation with a clock.

**Economic moat:** **NARROW, trending ERODING.** The moat type is *scale-cost* — LEG is the leading US innerspring/steel-rod-and-wire vertically integrated manufacturer (real share, real cost advantage in trade rod/wire where steel tariffs *widen* metal margins). But the evidence cuts against width: ROIC ~8% ≈ WACC (no economic profit), gross margin 18.1% only "stable" off a depressed base, revenue compounding *down* high-single-digits, and the company is *losing private-label content* to a customer's in-sourcing. A scale-cost moat that cannot prevent four years of revenue decline and zero economic profit is narrow and slowly eroding, not wide.

**Secular threat:** **MANAGEABLE, not terminal.** This is fundamentally a *cyclical* depression (housing affordability, existing-home-sales at multi-decade lows) compounded by a *channel-shift* in bedding — cheap imported/online mattresses dominating the low end (`retail-channel-shift`). But the import pressure is partially policy-reversible (tariffs/enforcement are a tailwind, not a tail), the steel/innerspring/auto-components franchises are not being technologically displaced, and the balance sheet is solvent. The cheapness (~5.4x P/FCF, 6.9% FCF yield) is therefore a **cyclical fear-print on a flat-to-eroding base** — not a melting-ice-cube value trap, but not a coiled spring either. The risk is *time*: the recovery may be deferred for years, and the multiple stays low because there is no economic profit and no shareholder return to wait on.
